perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall not, without the prior written approval of the Disclosing Party in each instance or unless otherwise expressly permitted in this Agreement, use for its own benefit, publish or otherwise disclose to others, or permit the use by others for their benefit or to the detriment of the Disclosing Party, any of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall endeavor not to, without what it deems to be reasonable prior approval of the Disclosing Party or unless vaguely permitted in this Agreement, use for its own benefit, publish or otherwise disclose to others if practical, or permit the use by others for their benefit or to the detriment of the Disclosing Party, any of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly prohibits the use, publication, or disclosure of confidential information without prior written approval. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'shall not' with 'shall endeavor not to,' suggesting a non-mandatory obligation. It further qualifies 'prior written approval' with 'what it deems to be reasonable,' making the approval process subjective. The addition of 'if practical' adds another layer of ambiguity, making the restriction on disclosure conditional. Finally, 'vaguely permitted' creates further ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.03 defines misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information through ambiguous wording, the modified text makes it more difficult to prove that a misappropriation occurred, as the 'endeavor' and 'if practical' clauses introduce wiggle room for the Receiving Party to argue they did not intentionally or directly violate the agreement, hindering enforcement under the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.03 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.03 325C.02 325C.04 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.03 Version List 325C.03 DAMAGES. (a) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (b) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under paragraph (a). History: 1980 c 594 s 5 ; 1987 c 1 s 2 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially hindering enforcement under Minnesota Statutes § 325C.03, which addresses damages for misappropriation. The original text imposes a clear prohibition, while the changed text uses terms like 'endeavor not to,' 'what it deems to be reasonable,' 'if practical,' and 'vaguely permitted,' creating loopholes. This makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation, as the receiving party can argue they did not intentionally or directly violate the agreement. This directly contradicts the intent of the statute to provide damages for misappropriation, as the ambiguity weakens the legal force of the contract. Snippet 1, referring to 325C.03, discusses damages for misappropriation. The changes introduce wiggle room for the Receiving Party to argue they did not intentionally or directly violate the agreement, hindering enforcement under the statute.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall carefully restrict access to the Confidential Information to those of its officers, directors and employees who clearly need such access in order to participate on behalf of the Receiving Party in the analysis and negotiation of a business relationship or any contract or agreement, or the advisability thereof, with the Disclosing Party and who are bound by written confidentiality agreements that protect third party information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall attempt to restrict access to the Confidential Information to those of its officers, directors and employees who might need such access in order to participate on behalf of the Receiving Party in the analysis and negotiation of a business relationship or any contract or agreement, or the general advisability thereof, with the Disclosing Party, and who are reasonably expected to adhere to confidentiality practices.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates that the Receiving Party 'carefully restrict' access to those who 'clearly need' it and who are 'bound by written confidentiality agreements.' The modified text softens this obligation by using 'shall attempt to restrict,' making the restriction less forceful. 'Clearly need' becomes 'might need,' broadening the scope of permissible access. Furthermore, the requirement of 'written confidentiality agreements' is replaced with 'reasonably expected to adhere to confidentiality practices,' which is vague and unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.02 allows for injunctive relief in cases of actual or threatened misappropriation. By weakening the access restrictions and confidentiality requirements, the modified text makes it more difficult for the Disclosing Party to demonstrate a 'threatened misappropriation' and obtain an injunction. The vagueness of the new language undermines the clarity required to prove the likelihood of harm, thus contradicting the intent and application of the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.02 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.02 325C.01 325C.03 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.02 Version List 325C.02 INJUNCTIVE RELIEF. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History: 1980 c 594 s 4 ; 1987 c 1 s 1 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the confidentiality requirements, making it more difficult to prove 'threatened misappropriation' and obtain an injunction under Minnesota Statutes § 325C.02, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text mandated 'carefully restrict' access and 'written confidentiality agreements,' while the changed text uses 'shall attempt to restrict' and 'reasonably expected to adhere to confidentiality practices,' which are less forceful and more vague. This directly contradicts the ability to seek injunctive relief for threatened misappropriation, as 'threatened misappropriation may be enjoined' according to Scraped Law Snippet 1.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if required by a judicial or governmental request, requirement or order; provided that the Receiving Party will take reasonable steps to give the Disclosing Party sufficient prior notice of such request, requirement or order for the Disclosing Party to contest, limit or protect such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if vaguely compelled by what appears to be a judicial or governmental request; provided that the Receiving Party may consider, if feasible, informing the Disclosing Party before such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a specific exception for disclosures required by judicial or governmental orders, but mandates 'reasonable steps' to provide prior notice. The modified text introduces several ambiguities. 'Vaguely compelled by what appears to be' weakens the requirement for a legitimate legal compulsion. Replacing 'request, requirement or order' with just 'request' diminishes the sense of legal obligation. Changing 'will take reasonable steps' to 'may consider, if feasible' makes the notice requirement entirely discretionary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45.03(a)(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45.03(a)(1) generally requires reasonable steps to avoid imposing undue burden or expense on a person subject to a subpoena. While not directly about confidential information, the modified clause introduces an element that could impede the disclosing party's right to challenge a subpoena, undermining the due process intended under the rule. The ambiguity of 'vaguely compelled' and the discretionary notice conflict with the spirit of the rule for subpoena challenges.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If the subpoena commands the production of documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or the inspection of premises before trial, then before it is served on

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that undermine the original contract's intent to provide notice for legal challenges. The original text mandates 'reasonable steps' for prior notice, while the modified text uses 'may consider, if feasible,' making notice discretionary. Although Snippet 1 concerns subpoenas, the Law Explanation extends this to the spirit of due process in challenging legal requests, which the 'vaguely compelled' clause and the discretionary notice undermine. This weakening directly contradicts the due process expectation of reasonable notice before disclosure as noted in the Law Explanation, thus creating a legal contradiction under the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall immediately return to the Disclosing Part y all written Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party and any and all records, notes and other written, printed or tangible materials pertaining to such Confidential Information upon receipt of a written request from the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall, when practical, return to the Disclosing Party what it subjectively believes to be written Confidential Information and what it considers to be relevant records upon receiving some form of communication from the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates an immediate return of all written confidential information upon written request. The modified text is significantly weaker. 'Shall immediately return' becomes 'shall, when practical, return', making the return conditional. 'All written Confidential Information' becomes 'what it subjectively believes to be written Confidential Information', allowing the Receiving Party to decide what constitutes confidential information. 'Any and all records, notes' becomes 'what it considers to be relevant records', again giving the Receiving Party discretion. Finally, 'upon receipt of a written request' is weakened to 'upon receiving some form of communication'.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.05 addresses the preservation of secrecy by court order. By significantly weakening the return obligation, the modified text increases the risk that confidential information will not be adequately preserved, potentially undermining any future legal proceedings aimed at protecting the trade secret and contradicting the purpose of the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.05 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.05 325C.04 325C.06 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.05 Version List 325C.05 PRESERVATION OF SECRECY. In an action under sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , a court shall preserve the secrecy of an alleged trade secret by reasonable means, which may include granting protective orders in connection with discovery proceedings, holding in-camera hearings, sealing the records of the action, and ordering any person involved in the litigation not to disclose an alleged trade secret without prior court approval. History: 1980 c 594 s 7 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates an immediate return of all confidential information, while the changed text weakens this obligation by making the return conditional ('when practical'), subjective ('what it subjectively believes'), and alters the method of communication ('some form of communication' instead of 'written request'). Minnesota Statute 325C.05 states that courts 'shall preserve the secrecy of an alleged trade secret by reasonable means'. By significantly weakening the obligation to return confidential information, the modified text increases the risk that confidential information will not be adequately preserved, potentially undermining any future legal proceedings aimed at protecting the trade secret. This weakening directly contradicts the statute's intent to preserve secrecy, as the receiving party now has considerable discretion over what and when to return, thus creating an Ambiguities Legal Contradiction, as defined.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws of th e State of Minnesota.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be somewhat guided by and generally understood in light of the spirit of the laws of the State of Minnesota.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes Minnesota law as the governing law, providing legal certainty. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'governed by and construed in accordance with' with 'somewhat guided by and generally understood in light of the spirit of the laws.' This weakens the binding nature of Minnesota law, suggesting it's merely a suggestion or interpretation guide rather than a definitive legal framework. The term 'spirit of the laws' is also vague and open to subjective interpretation, making it difficult to enforce the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 541.05 establishes various statutes of limitations for different causes of action. By weakening the governing law provision, the modified text creates uncertainty about which state's laws (including statutes of limitations) apply. This ambiguity could significantly impact the ability to bring a timely claim, contradicting the certainty that the statute of limitations aims to provide.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 541.05 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes CIVIL PROCEDURE Chapter 541 Section 541.05 541.04 541.051 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 541 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 541.05 Version List Recent History 2015 Subd. 1 Amended 2015 c 5 art 15 s 18 2000 Subd. 1 Amended 2000 c 471 s 1 541.05 VARIOUS CASES, SIX YEARS. § Subdivision 1. Six-year limitation. Except where the Uniform Commercial Code otherwise prescribes, the following actions shall be commenced within six years: (1) upon a contract or other obligation, express or implied, as to which no other limitation is expressly prescribed; (2) upon a liability created by statute, other than those arising upon a penalty or forfeiture or where a shorter period is provided by section 541.07 ; (3) for a trespass upon real estate; (4) for taking, detaining, or injuring personal property, including actions for the specific recovery thereof; (5) for criminal conversation, or for any other injury to the person or rights of another, not arising on contract, and not hereinafter enumerated; (6) for relief on the ground of fraud, in which case the cause of action shall not be deemed to have accrued until the discovery by the aggrieved party of the facts constituting the fraud; (7) against sureties upon the official bond of any public officer, whether of the state or of any county, town, school district, or a municipality therein; in which case the limitation shall not begin to run until the term of such officer for which the bond was given shall have expired; (8) for damages caused by a dam, used for commercial purposes; or (9) for assault, battery, false impriso
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the governing law provision, creating uncertainty about which state's laws apply. The original text mandates Minnesota law, while the modified text only suggests Minnesota law as a guide. This directly contradicts the certainty intended by Minnesota Statutes § 541.05, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Statute 541.05 sets the statute of limitations. By introducing ambiguity in the governing law, the altered text makes it difficult to determine the applicable statute of limitations, affecting the ability to bring a timely claim.
